perturbation.original_text: Article 14 – Dispute Resolution This Agreement is governed by, and shall be construed in accordance with the laws of France. Any dispute arising from or in connection with this Agreement shall be resolved by both Parties or their representatives, if any, through consultation and negotiations at the request of either Party by written notice to the other Party. Failing a resolution within three weeks following the notice mentioned in the above paragraph, the Tribunal de Grande Instance de Paris shall have exclusive jurisdiction to hear and decide any suit, action or proceedings, and to settle any disputes arising out of or in connection with this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Article 5 – Refusal Nothing in this Agreement shall obligate the ICC Court to disclose any information to the Member. ICC has full discretion to determine which Confidential Information or Proprietary Information can be disclosed to the Member during the term of office of the Member. Article 14 – Dispute Resolution This Agreement is governed by, and shall be construed in accordance with the laws of France. Any dispute arising from or in connection with this Agreement shall be resolved by both Parties or their representatives, if any, through consultation and negotiations at the request of either Party by written notice to the other Party. Failing a resolution within three weeks following the notice mentioned in the above paragraph.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the section that mentions exclusive jurisdiction of the Tribunal de Grande Instance de Paris to another section and truncating it makes it difficult to enforce, as the agreement to the said jurisdiction is not clearly stated.
perturbation.contradicted_law: French Code of Civil Procedure, Article 48
perturbation.law_citation: Article 48 of the French Code of Civil Procedure
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 48 of the French Code of Civil Procedure requires clear and express agreement for a jurisdiction clause to be valid. By removing half of the original text, the explicit agreement to the Tribunal de Grande Instance de Paris' jurisdiction becomes unclear, making the clause potentially unenforceable under French law.

perturbation.justification: The modified text removes a critical component of the dispute resolution clause, specifically the agreed-upon jurisdiction of the Tribunal de Grande Instance de Paris. The Law Explanation asserts that Article 48 of the French Code of Civil Procedure requires a clear agreement for a jurisdiction clause to be valid. By truncating the clause, the explicit agreement to the Tribunal's jurisdiction is obscured, potentially rendering the clause unenforceable. This aligns with the 'Structural Flaws Legal Contradiction' definition, where a required legal disclosure is placed in an irrelevant or misleading section, making it difficult to interpret.
